did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses ensembles of convolutional neural networks (MobileNet V2, DenseNet 169, and EfficientNet B2) for the classification and detection of malignant skin lesions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, and melanocytic nevi) using decision fusion techniques.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing two new ensemble neural network models (Ensemble Model 1 and Ensemble Model 2) for detecting malignant skin lesions. It details specific methodologies (MobileNet V2, DenseNet 169, EfficientNet B2), describes the training and testing process on a dataset of 2613 images, and reports original performance results (e.g., 91.04% global accuracy for Ensemble Model 2). The structure includes standard research sections such as Abstract, Introduction, Methods, Results, and Conclusion, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Detection of Malignant Skin Lesions Based on Decision Fusion of Ensembles of Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study proposes two ensemble neural network models for the detection and classification of four malignant skin lesion types: basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, benign keratosis, and melanocytic nevi. The models utilize MobileNet V2, DenseNet 169, and EfficientNet B2 architectures, trained on a dataset derived from HAM10000 and ISIC Challenge 2019 archives containing 2613 images. The first system employs weighted voting for global prediction, while the second system fuses decisions from six binary classification subsystems per network. Evaluation results demonstrate that the second system achieves a superior global accuracy of 91.04% compared to 89.62% for the first system. The research highlights that individual performance metrics, such as F1 scores, vary significantly depending on the specific skin lesion class.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four primary classes used in the classification task. Described as the most lethal type of skin cancer. Included in the dataset with 867 test images.
One of the four primary classes used in the classification task. Described as the least aggressive type of non-melanoma cancer. Included in the dataset with 485 test images.

One of the four primary classes used in the classification task. Described as a generic class including three biologically similar subclasses. Included in the dataset with 342 test images. Identified as the weakest performing class in terms of F1 score.

One of the four primary classes used in the classification task. Described as benign neoplasms. Included in the dataset with 919 test images. Identified as the best performing class in terms of F1 score.
Mentioned as a class existing in the original HAM10000/ISIC dataset but completely abandoned for this study due to lower image counts compared to the selected four classes.
Mentioned as a class existing in the original HAM10000/ISIC dataset but completely abandoned for this study.
Mentioned as a class existing in the original HAM10000/ISIC dataset but completely abandoned for this study.

all_task_types.target: Four discrete skin lesion classes: basal cell carcinoma (bcc), melanoma (mel), benign keratosis (bkl), and melanocytic nevi (nv).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of malignant skin lesions. It proposes two ensemble models: Ensemble Model 1 uses weighted voting for global prediction across four classes, and Ensemble Model 2 uses a fusion of 18 binary classification subsystems (one for each pair of classes) to predict the final class. The task is explicitly defined as classification, with performance measured by accuracy, F1 score, sensitivity, specificity, and precision.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 and ISIC Challenge 2019
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset composed of medical images collected from the HAM10000 [22] and ISIC Challenge 2019 [23] archives. The original dataset contained seven classes, but only four were retained for this study due to larger image counts. Duplicate images and lesions with multiple images were removed. Approximately 20% of the nv (melanocytic nevi) images were randomly discarded to address class imbalance, and image augmentation was applied to the mel, bcc, and bkl classes.

use.training: Used for training the MobileNet V2, DenseNet 169, and EfficientNet B2 neural networks after augmentation. The dataset was split into 75% for training.
use.validation: Used for validation (15% of total images) to monitor loss and accuracy metrics during training.
use.testing: Used for testing the final ensemble models (10% of total images). The test set consisted of 2613 images.
use.other: The original HAM10000/ISIC dataset contained additional classes (akiec, df, vasc) which were completely abandoned for this study.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to evaluate model performance in the validation phase and to calculate F1 scores, which were subsequently used as weights for the ensemble models.
Calculated for each individual neural network and class to serve as associated weights for the decision fusion mechanism in Ensemble Model 1 and Ensemble Model 2.
Used to fuse decisions from constituent neural networks (Ensemble Model 1) or binary classification subsystems (Ensemble Model 2) to generate global predictions.
Calculated for individual classifiers and the ensemble during the validation phase to compare performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy and F1 scores without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000 and ISIC Challenge 2019.experiment: Ensemble Model 2 (Binary Models with Multiple Weights) - Global System
HAM10000 and ISIC Challenge 2019.notes: The global accuracy of Ensemble Model 2 (91.04%) is superior to Ensemble Model 1 (89.62%). Individual F1 scores for each class and the global system varied from 81.36% to 94.17%.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of MobileNet V2, DenseNet 169, and EfficientNet B2

ensemble_configurations.name: Ensemble Model 1
ensemble_configurations.description: Direct decisions of each NN multiplied by associated weights (F1 score based). 3 networks x 4 classes.

ensemble_configurations.name: Ensemble Model 2
ensemble_configurations.description: Fusion of 18 binary classification subsystems (one for each pair of classes) for each network. 3 networks x 6 binary pairs.

backbone_details.MobileNet V2: Uses depthwise separable convolution (bottleneck layers) to reduce complexity and memory.
backbone_details.DenseNet 169: Used as a feature extractor in some contexts, part of the ensemble.
backbone_details.EfficientNet B2: Uses scaling by combining depth, width, and resolution.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named, but Keras/TensorFlow framework used
training_details.learning_rate: Initial 0.01, halved if validation metrics (loss/accuracy) did not improve for two consecutive epochs
training_details.epochs: Not explicitly reported, but training took approximately 30 hours for the 18 binary models in Ensemble Model 2
training_details.validation_strategy: Best model from one epoch to another retained based on validation metrics
training_details.data_augmentation: Applied to mel, bcc, and bkl classes; 20% of nv images discarded to address imbalance

details.Ensemble Model 1: Weighted summation of global predictions from 3 networks for 4 classes. Weights derived from F1 scores.
details.Ensemble Model 2: Weighted summation of binary classification subsystems. Final decision taken by maximum SCORE value among the four classes.

preprocessing_techniques: Removal of duplicate images and lesions with multiple images from the HAM10000 and ISIC Challenge 2019 archives
Random discarding of approximately 20% of melanocytic nevi (nv) images to address class imbalance
Image augmentation applied to melanoma (mel), basal cell carcinoma (bcc), and benign keratosis (bkl) classes
Resizing of input images to 224 × 224 × 3 pixels
Abandonment of akiec, df, and vasc classes due to lower image counts

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the melanoma (mel), basal cell carcinoma (bcc), and benign keratosis (bkl) classes to balance the dataset. The specific augmentation methods (e.g., geometric or color transforms) are not detailed in the text, but the paper cites reference [24] for the method used. Approximately 20% of melanocytic nevi (nv) images were randomly discarded to address class imbalance rather than being augmented.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble model based on the fusion of intermediate binary classification subsystems (Ensemble Model 2) achieved a global accuracy of 91.04%, which is superior to the classic system based on weighted voting of constituent networks (Ensemble Model 1) that achieved 89.62%.
Individual performance indicators, such as the F1 score, vary significantly depending on the specific type of skin lesion, ranging from 81.36% to 94.17% across the four classes.
Among the four studied lesion types, melanocytic nevi (nv) was the best-performing class, while benign keratosis (bkl) was the weakest performing class in terms of F1 score.

The final ensemble for multiclass classification yields weaker results than the individual binary branches, which is attributed to the increased complexity of distinguishing between four classes compared to two.
The study confirms that early detection of malignant skin lesions, particularly melanoma, is critical for patient survival, and AI-based decision fusion offers a viable support system for this task.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a small test set of only 2,613 images, which may restrict the generalizability of the results.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with benign keratosis (bkl) having the fewest test images (342) and melanocytic nevi (nv) the most (919), leading to poor performance for the bkl class (F1 score 81.36%).
Three classes from the original HAM10000/ISIC dataset (akiec, df, vasc) were completely abandoned due to lower image counts, potentially limiting the clinical scope of the model.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to validate the significance of performance differences between models.
Performance varies significantly by lesion type, with the weakest class (bkl) having an F1 score of 81.36% and the best (nv) at 94.17%, indicating inconsistent reliability across classes.
The global ensemble performance (91.04% accuracy) is weaker than the individual binary classification branches, attributed to the increased complexity of distinguishing four classes versus two.

Data leakage risks were mitigated by removing duplicate images and lesions with multiple images, but the random discarding of 20% of nv images to address imbalance is a notable preprocessing step that reduces data availability.
The study relies on a single dataset (HAM10000/ISIC Challenge 2019) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting evidence of real-world clinical applicability.
